## SUB-PFCS-001 — Aircraft-Specific Checklist Selection
Statement: The PFCS shall automatically present the correct checklist for the specific aircraft type and configuration assigned to each flight, without requiring the flight crew to manually select or modify the checklist template.
Rationale: Presenting an incorrect or generic checklist introduces the risk that items specific to a particular aircraft variant or configuration are missed, defeating the purpose of a standardized pre-flight check.
Acceptance criteria:
- The checklist presented matches the aircraft type code and configuration record held in the dispatch system for that flight.
- If the aircraft type or configuration changes after checklist presentation has begun, the system detects the mismatch and prompts the crew to reload the correct checklist.
- No crew action beyond opening the assigned flight is required to retrieve the correct checklist.
Verification: Assign three flights each with different aircraft type and configuration combinations; confirm that the checklist rendered in each case matches the authoritative template for that combination.

---

## SUB-PFCS-002 — Explicit Item Confirmation
Statement: The PFCS shall require each checklist item to be explicitly confirmed by the flight crew before it is recorded as complete, preventing bulk or implicit completion of items.
Rationale: Paper-based practices allow crews to sign off an entire card at once; individual confirmation ensures each item receives deliberate attention and produces a reliable completion record.
Acceptance criteria:
- Each item presents a discrete confirm action that must be performed; no item is marked complete unless that action is taken.
- Bulk-completion controls that would mark multiple unreviewed items complete simultaneously are absent from the interface.
- An item that has not received an explicit confirmation is displayed in a visually distinct incomplete state throughout the session.
Verification: Attempt to complete a checklist by interacting with only a subset of items; verify that all untouched items remain in the incomplete state and that no aggregate completion action is available.

---

## SUB-PFCS-003 — Exception Flagging with Documented Reason
Statement: The PFCS shall allow a flight crew member to flag any checklist item as an exception instead of confirming it, and shall require a reason to be recorded before the flag is accepted.
Rationale: Operational conditions occasionally make a checklist item not applicable or require a deferred action; a structured exception path with a mandatory reason preserves safety traceability while accommodating legitimate deviations.
Acceptance criteria:
- The flag action is available on every mandatory item and cannot be submitted without a non-empty reason entry.
- A flagged item is displayed distinctly from both confirmed and incomplete items throughout the rest of the session.
- The recorded reason is stored as part of the tamper-evident flight record and is visible to safety reviewers.
Verification: Flag a mandatory item without entering a reason and confirm submission is blocked; then flag the same item with a reason and confirm it is accepted, stored, and correctly displayed.

---

## SUB-PFCS-004 — Ready-for-Pushback Status Withholding
Statement: The PFCS shall withhold the "ready for pushback" status for a flight until every mandatory checklist item is either explicitly confirmed or carries a captain-authorized override, and shall never issue that status while any item remains in an incomplete or unresolved state.
Rationale: The core safety objective of the system is to prevent pushback with an incomplete checklist; the status gate is the enforcement mechanism that closes the gap identified in the legacy paper process.
Acceptance criteria:
- The "ready for pushback" status is not issued while one or more mandatory items are in the incomplete state.
- Completing the final outstanding mandatory item causes the status to be issued immediately without additional crew action.
- Attempting to retrieve pushback status from the dispatch integration endpoint while items are outstanding returns a negative response and the count of unresolved items.
Verification: Execute a checklist leaving one mandatory item incomplete; confirm the status is withheld. Confirm the item; confirm the status is then issued. Query the dispatch endpoint at each stage and verify consistent responses.

---

## SUB-PFCS-005 — Captain Override with Documentation
Statement: The PFCS shall allow only a user authenticated in the captain role to authorize an override of the "ready for pushback" status gate, and shall require the captain to enter a documented justification before the override takes effect.
Rationale: Operational necessity may require departure with an unresolved item; restricting override authority to the captain and mandating justification maintains accountability and ensures overrides are deliberate rather than accidental.
Acceptance criteria:
- Override controls are not presented to users authenticated in any role other than captain.
- A captain override attempt without a justification entry is blocked by the system.
- An authorized override is stored in the tamper-evident record linked to the captain's user identity, timestamp, and the specific items overridden.
Verification: Attempt override as a dispatcher and as a first officer; confirm controls are unavailable. Attempt override as captain without justification; confirm it is blocked. Complete override as captain with justification; confirm storage and linkage in the record.

---

## SUB-PFCS-006 — Per-Item Crew Attribution and Timestamping
Statement: The PFCS shall record the identity of the crew member who confirmed or flagged each checklist item and the time at which that action was taken, as part of the flight's permanent record.
Rationale: Attribution supports post-event investigation and operational review; without it, records cannot establish who performed each check or whether the sequence was operationally valid.
Acceptance criteria:
- Every confirmed or flagged item in the stored record carries the authenticated user identifier of the acting crew member and a timestamp accurate to within one second.
- Items confirmed by different crew members in the same session are attributed to their respective users, not to a single session owner.
- Attribution data is included in the record exported for safety reviewer access.
Verification: Have two differently authenticated crew members each confirm distinct items in the same checklist session; inspect the stored record and confirm correct individual attribution and timestamps for each item.

---

## SUB-PFCS-007 — Tamper-Evident Record Retention
Statement: The PFCS shall retain a tamper-evident record of the completed checklist for every flight, such that any post-storage alteration is detectable by operations and safety staff.
Rationale: Safety investigations and regulatory audits require confidence that records reflect what actually occurred; records that can be silently modified are inadmissible as evidence of compliance.
Acceptance criteria:
- Each finalized flight record is sealed with a cryptographic integrity mechanism upon closure of the checklist session.
- Any modification to a sealed record, whether to item status, attribution, timestamps, or override data, causes an integrity check failure that is surfaced to reviewers.
- Records are retained for a minimum period defined in the system configuration and cannot be deleted through normal user-facing controls.
Verification: Retrieve a sealed record, manually alter a field in the data store, re-run the integrity check, and confirm a failure is reported. Attempt deletion through user-facing controls and confirm it is blocked.

---

## SUB-PFCS-008 — Dispatch System Integration for Release Control
Statement: The PFCS shall expose an integration interface to the dispatch system that returns the current pushback-readiness status of any flight, and the dispatch system shall be unable to release a flight for pushback unless that interface returns a confirmed-complete status.
Rationale: Independent status control in PFCS is only effective if the dispatch release workflow is gated on it; without integration, a dispatcher could release a flight while the checklist remains open.
Acceptance criteria:
- The integration interface returns a machine-readable status of complete or incomplete for a queried flight identifier.
- The dispatch system, when queried for a flight with incomplete status, cannot progress that flight to a pushback-released state.
- The interface responds within two seconds under normal network conditions.
Verification: Query the interface for a flight in each status state; verify correct responses. Attempt to release an incomplete flight through the dispatch system and confirm the action is blocked.

---

## SUB-PFCS-009 — Dispatcher Alerting for Overrides and Flagged Items
Statement: The PFCS shall automatically send an alert to the assigned dispatcher whenever a captain authorizes an override or a mandatory checklist item is flagged as an exception, before the affected flight's checklist session is closed.
Rationale: Dispatchers are responsible for flight release decisions; real-time notification of deviations ensures they can act on the information rather than discovering it after pushback.
Acceptance criteria:
- An alert is delivered to the dispatcher within thirty seconds of an override authorization or a mandatory-item flag being saved.
- The alert identifies the flight, the specific item involved, and the reason or justification recorded.
- If the dispatcher is not actively logged in, the alert is queued and presented upon next login with its original timestamp preserved.
Verification: Trigger an override and a flag on separate items with a dispatcher logged out; log the dispatcher in and confirm both queued alerts are present with correct content and timestamps.

---

## SUB-PFCS-010 — Role-Based Access Control
Statement: The PFCS shall enforce role-based access such that flight crew, dispatchers, maintenance personnel, and safety reviewers can each access only the functions and records appropriate to their defined role.
Rationale: Unrestricted access creates risk of unauthorized modifications, accidental or deliberate, to checklists and records; role separation is a prerequisite for the system's integrity guarantees.
Acceptance criteria:
- A user authenticated as a dispatcher cannot confirm, flag, or override checklist items.
- A user authenticated as a maintenance role can access and update maintenance-relevant items but cannot alter flight crew confirmations.
- A user authenticated as a safety reviewer has read-only access to all flight records and cannot modify any stored data.
Verification: Authenticate a test account in each role and attempt all actions defined as out-of-scope for that role; confirm each attempt is blocked and the attempted action is logged.

---

## SUB-PFCS-011 — Offline Operation
Statement: The PFCS shall remain fully usable for checklist presentation, item confirmation, flagging, override authorization, and record creation when no network connectivity is available, without any degradation of required functionality.
Rationale: Ramp and gate environments have intermittent connectivity; any capability that becomes unavailable offline creates pressure on crews to defer or skip checklist steps, recreating the safety gap the system is designed to close.
Acceptance criteria:
- All checklist functions operate without error when the device has no network connection, using locally cached checklist templates current to within the last completed sync.
- Records created offline are stored locally in the same tamper-evident format used for online records.
- The interface clearly indicates offline status to the crew but does not block or restrict any required checklist action on that basis.
Verification: Disable all network interfaces on a test device, complete a full checklist session including an override, and confirm all data is stored locally with integrity seals intact and no functionality errors reported.

---

## SUB-PFCS-012 — Offline Record Reconciliation
Statement: The PFCS shall automatically reconcile all locally stored records created during offline operation with the central data store once connectivity is restored, without requiring crew intervention and without overwriting or discarding any locally created data.
Rationale: Offline records have no safety or audit value unless they reliably reach the central store; manual sync steps introduce the risk of records being lost or forgotten, particularly during high-tempo turnaround operations.
Acceptance criteria:
- Reconciliation begins automatically within sixty seconds of network connectivity being detected, without any user action.
- Every record created offline is transferred to the central store and verifiable there, including tamper-evident seals, attribution, and timestamps, within five minutes of sync initiation under normal network conditions.
- Any conflict detected during reconciliation, such as a duplicate flight identifier, is flagged to an operations administrator rather than silently resolved, and neither conflicting record is deleted.
Verification: Create checklist records offline for two flights, restore connectivity, wait five minutes, and confirm both records appear in the central store with intact integrity seals and original timestamps; introduce a synthetic conflict and confirm it surfaces to the administrator queue.

### EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

Auth-service migration is 60% complete with three of five microservices successfully transitioned to the new identity provider. The project remains on budget and on track to complete full cutover in four weeks.

**Moderate-High.** We have clear visibility on remaining work and the source of the timeline extension. The team has demonstrated execution capability, and QA sign-off on completed work is solid. The main dependency — the rollback rehearsal before billing migration — is a process milestone we control.
